skill.name: animation-shader
skill.description: READ this skill when implementing or configuring animation-style shaders (Toon/Cel Shaders) — including outlines, rim lighting, toon shading, MatCap, emission, dissolve, hatching, or any stylized rendering effect. Contains preset styles and feature-to-reference mappings for lilToon, Poiyomi, UTS2, RToon, SToon, and ToonShadingCollection. Works as a domain knowledge plugin alongside workflow skills (OpenSpec, SpecKit) or plan mode of an agent.
skill.summary: Domain knowledge reference for animation-style shaders with preset styles and feature mappings for Unity shader libraries
skill.author: Yuki001

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 47 files (4040 lines) of shader documentation. All static findings are false positives triggered by markdown documentation syntax and HLSL shader code examples. The skill contains no executable code, shell commands, network requests, or credential access. Risk factors indicate this is a pure documentation/reference skill for Unity toon shaders.

content.user_title: Create Animation-Style Shaders for Unity Games
content.value_statement: Building stylized toon shaders for Unity requires understanding complex shader parameters and feature mappings. This skill provides preset styles and detailed references for six popular shader libraries, helping you implement anime, cel-shaded, and artistic rendering effects.
actual_capabilities: Maps user requests to preset shader styles (Basic Anime, Advanced Illustration, Cyber VFX, etc.)
Provides detailed feature references for six shader libraries: lilToon, Poiyomi, RToon, SToon, UnityChanToonShader, ToonShadingCollection
Explains core shading concepts including 3-tone shading, shadow ramps, outline rendering, and rim lighting
Documents advanced effects like emission, dissolve, MatCap, hatching, halftone, and vertex manipulation
Contains HLSL shader code examples showing implementation patterns for each feature
Offers workflow guidance for standalone use or integration with OpenSpec/SpecKit workflow skills
limitations: Does not execute or generate shader code directly
Requires manual implementation of shader settings in Unity Editor
Does not provide debugging assistance for shader compilation errors
Shader library support limited to the six documented collections

use_cases.title: Game Developer Choosing a Shader Style
use_cases.description: A game developer wants to add a distinctive anime aesthetic to their VRChat avatar. They use the skill to understand the difference between Basic Anime and Advanced Illustration presets, ultimately selecting a style that balances visual quality with performance.
use_cases.target_user: Game developers working on VRChat, Roblox, or indie Unity games

use_cases.title: Technical Artist Configuring Rim Lighting
use_cases.description: A technical artist needs to implement rim lighting across multiple characters with consistent results. They reference the rim light documentation to understand the differences between lilToon and Poiyomi implementations, choosing the approach that best matches their existing workflow.

use_cases.title: Beginner Learning Toon Shading Concepts
use_cases.description: A beginner learning game art wants to understand the difference between 3-tone shading, shadow ramps, and shading grade maps. They use the skill as a structured introduction to toon shading theory, progressing from Basic Anime to Semi-Realistic Toon presets.
use_cases.target_user: Game art students and hobbyist developers

prompt_templates.title: Style Selection Query
prompt_templates.prompt: I want to create [describe visual style] for my Unity game. Should I use Basic Anime, Advanced Illustration, or create a custom style? What features do I need?
prompt_templates.scenario: When a user is unsure about which shader preset to start with

prompt_templates.title: Feature Implementation Reference
prompt_templates.prompt: How do I implement [rim light / outline / emission / dissolve] in [lilToon / Poiyomi / RToon]? Show me the relevant settings and any code patterns.
prompt_templates.scenario: When a user knows what feature they want but needs implementation details

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Shader Feature Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare how [shadow ramp] works in RToon versus SToon. What are the tradeoffs for performance and visual quality?
prompt_templates.scenario: When choosing between shader libraries for a specific feature

prompt_templates.title: Complete Character Setup Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through setting up a complete anime-style character with [Base Color, 3-tone shading, outline, rim light]. List all the files I need to read and the order to configure them.
prompt_templates.scenario: When a user needs a comprehensive implementation plan

output_examples.input: How do I create an outline effect in lilToon?
output_examples.output: For lilToon outlines, use the following reference: 1) Read references/lilToon/Details/08_Outline.md for outline settings, 2) Key properties include: Outline Width, Outline Color, Outline Detail, and Outline Vertex Normal. For best results, use the Inverted Hull method with Outline Width of 0.05-0.1 for VRChat avatars. Combine with 3-tone shading from references/lilToon/Details/02_Lighting_Shadows.md for authentic anime aesthetics.

output_examples.input: What's the difference between MatCap and Rim Light?
output_examples.output: MatCap (Material Capture) projects a spherical texture based on view angle, ideal for reflection effects without real-time calculation. Rim Light adds light at grazing angles for stylized edge highlighting. For VRChat performance, MatCap is often preferred over full PBR reflections. See references/RToon/Details/04_MatCap_Reflection.md and references/lilToon/Details/03_Surface_Reflections.md for implementation details.

output_examples.input: Help me choose between Poiyomi and lilToon for a mobile game
output_examples.output: For mobile game development: lilToon is recommended for performance-critical VRChat worlds or mobile games, featuring optimized shaders with essential toon features. Poiyomi offers more advanced features including audio-reactive effects but has higher rendering costs. Consider: Basic Anime preset works well on mobile with lilToon, while Advanced Illustration style may require performance testing. Review Poiyomi Features.md and lilToon Features.md for complete capability comparison.

best_practices: Always read ALL reference files listed for a feature, not just one shader library's documentation
Start with the Basic Anime preset when learning, then customize toward your target aesthetic
Consider VRChat shader complexity limits: use simplified 3-tone shading over PBR when performance matters
anti_patterns: Do not mix outline methods (Inverted Hull vs. Vertex Expansion) without understanding their visual differences
Avoid enabling too many effects simultaneously: prioritize 3-tone shading + outline + one accent feature
Do not skip reading the Features.md files before diving into Details documentation

faq.question: Which shader library should I start with as a beginner?
faq.answer: Start with lilToon if you want the easiest learning curve and best VRChat compatibility. Start with Poiyomi if you need advanced features like audio reactivity. Both have extensive documentation in this skill.

faq.question: Can I use this skill to generate actual shader code?
faq.answer: No, this skill provides documentation and reference materials. For code generation, combine it with a coding skill like OpenSpec that can write shader code based on the parameters documented here.

faq.question: How do I optimize shaders for VRChat performance?
faq.answer: Use simplified shading: 3-tone instead of PBR, baked lighting where possible, disable expensive features like dissolution on avatar shaders. Refer to Basic Anime and Semi-Realistic presets for optimization patterns.

faq.question: What's the difference between 3-tone shading and shadow ramps?
faq.answer: 3-tone shading uses two shadow thresholds for hard transitions. Shadow ramps use a gradient texture for softer, more painterly transitions. Both are documented in the lighting references for each shader.

faq.question: Can I export shader settings from one library to another?
faq.answer: Not directly, as each shader has unique parameters. This skill helps you understand equivalent features across libraries so you can recreate similar looks. The cross-library comparisons in presets guide you through finding the right equivalents.

faq.question: How do I add dissolve or emission effects?
faq.answer: Dissolve and emission are advanced features documented in the Special Effects sections. Dissolve requires a noise texture and is performance-intensive. Emission can be simple (always-on) or animated. Check references/lilToon/Details/04_Special_Effects.md or the equivalent Poiyomi documentation.
